metadata.title: Gender differences in lipid and glucose kinetics during short-term fasting
metadata.journal: American Journal of Physiology - Endocrinology and Metabolism
metadata.study_design: Experimental study with stable isotope labeled tracers measuring whole body glycerol and glucose kinetics at 14 and 22 hours of fasting
metadata.demographics: Six men (32 ± 4 yr old) and six premenopausal women (27 ± 2 yr old), matched on percent body fat (24±2% and 23±2% body weight as fat, respectively)

evidence.quote: Gender differences in lipid and glucose kinetics during short-term fasting
BETTINA MITTENDORFER, JEFFREY F. HOROWITZ, AND SAMUEL KLEIN
Am J Physiol Endocrinol Metab 281: E1333–E1339, 2001
Six women and six men who were matched for adiposity (24±2 and 23±2% body wt as fat, respectively)
Substrate kinetics were measured by infusing stable isotope labeled tracers of glucose ([2H2]glucose) and glycerol ([2H5]glycerol)
Six men (32 ± 4 yr old) and six premenopausal women (27 ± 2 yr old)

methods_participants.label: Participant N and demographics
methods_participants.value: Six men (32 ± 4 yr old) and six premenopausal women (27 ± 2 yr old), matched on percent body fat

evidence.quote: Six men (32 6 4 yr old) and six premenopausal women (27 6 2 yr old), who were matched on percent body fat, participated in this study

methods_participants.label: Inclusion criteria - health status
methods_participants.value: All subjects considered to be in good health after comprehensive medical evaluation including history, physical examination, electrocardiogram, and standard blood and urine tests

evidence.quote: All subjects were considered to be in good health after completing a comprehensive medical evaluation that included a history and physical examination, an electrocardiogram, and standard blood and urine tests

methods_participants.label: Inclusion criteria - medications and smoking
methods_participants.value: In female subjects, the study was performed during the first 2 weeks of the follicular phase of their menstrual cycle

evidence.quote: None of the subjects was taking regular medications or smoked tobacco

methods_participants.label: Inclusion criteria - body weight stability
methods_participants.value: All subjects had stable body weight for ≥2 months and had been sedentary (regular exercise <1 h/wk) for ≥6 months before the study

evidence.quote: All subjects had a stable body weight for $2 mo and had been sedentary (regular exercise ,1h/wk)for$6mobeforethestudy
Each subject's fat mass (FM) and fat-free mass (FFM) were determined by dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (Hologic QDR1000/w, Waltham, MA)

methods_participants.label: Pre-study meal timing and composition
methods_participants.value: At 1800, subjects consumed a standard meal containing 12 kcal/kg body weight (55% carbohydrate, 30% fat, 15% protein)

evidence.quote: At 1800, subjects consumed a standard meal containing 12 kcal/kg body wt (55% of total energy as carbohydrate, 30% as fat, and 15% as protein)

methods_participants.label: Blood sampling timing - 14 hour fast
methods_participants.value: At 0530, catheters were inserted into a forearm vein for isotope infusion and into a radial artery for blood sampling

evidence.quote: At 0530, catheters were inserted into a forearm vein for isotope infusion and into a radial artery for blood sampling

methods_participants.label: Blood sample collection tubes - fatty acid and glucose

evidence.quote: Plasma insulin (13) and glucagon (15) concentrations were measured by radioimmunoassay

methods_participants.label: Glycerol and glucose tracer-to-tracee ratio determination
methods_participants.value: GC-MS (MSD 5973 system with capillary column; Hewlett-Packard) with HFB anhydride derivative formation and EI ionization

evidence.quote: Plasma glycerol and glucose TTR were determined by GC-MS (MSD 5973 system with capillary column; Hewlett-Packard) as previously described (22,23,40). Briefly, plasma proteins were precipitated with ice-cold acetone, and hexane was used to extract plasma lipids... Heptafluorobutyric (HFB) anhydride was used to form an HFB derivative of glycerol and glucose, and ions were produced by EI ionization

methods_participants.label: Statistical analysis method
methods_participants.value: Two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with gender and time as factors, followed by post hoc comparisons with Student's t-test for multiple comparisons

evidence.quote: A two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA), with gender and time as factors, was used to test for significant differences in substrate kinetics between men and women during fasting. Significant F-ratios were followed by post hoc comparisons with the use of Student's t-test for multiple comparisons... A value of P #0.05 was considered to be statistically significant

methods_participants.label: Subject positioning during study
methods_participants.value: Subjects remained in bed for the entire duration of the study

evidence.quote: Subjects remained in bed for the entire duration of the study

methods_participants.label: Plasma separation timing
methods_participants.value: Plasma was separated by centrifugation within 30 minutes of collection

evidence.quote: plasma was separated by centrifugation within 30 min of collection

methods_participants.label: Plasma storage temperature
methods_participants.value: Plasma was stored at -70°C until final analyses were performed

evidence.quote: Plasma was stored at 270°C until final analyses were performed

narrative_results.statement: Basal glycerol rate of appearance in plasma was greater in women than in men

evidence.quote: Basal glycerol rate of appearance (R_a) in plasma, an indicator of whole body lipolytic rate, was greater in women than in men (2.1±0.2 vs. 1.5±0.1 mmol·kg body wt⁻¹·min⁻¹; P < 0.05)

narrative_results.statement: Sexual dimorphism exists in lipid, but not glucose, metabolism during both basal and short-term fasting conditions

evidence.quote: These data demonstrate the presence of sexual dimorphism in lipid, but not glucose, metabolism during both basal and short-term fasting conditions

discrepancies.pass_2_value: Pass 1 only mentions body weight stability; Pass 2 adds exercise sedentary requirement that is missing from Pass 1's extraction of this criterion
Pass 1 describes exercise level criteria while Pass 2 describes menstrual cycle phase criteria - these are completely different inclusion criteria that should not be paired

Pass_1 specifies epinephrine is twice as high in women at 14h fasting. Pass_2 states 'mean basal' epinephrine without time specification. These describe different conditions (14h fasting vs basal), creating a factual conflict.

structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Plasma glycerol and glucose TTR were determined by GC-MS (MSD 5973 system with capillary column; Hewlett-Packard) as previously described (22,23,40). Briefly, plasma proteins were precipitated with ice-cold acetone, and hexane was used to extract plasma lipids. The aqueous phase, containing glycerol and glucose, was dried by speed-vac centrifugation (Savant Instruments, Farmingdale, NY). Heptafluorobutyric (HFB) anhydride was used to form an HFB derivative of glycerol and glucose
Glycerol and glucose TTR assay method
GC-MS
GC-MS (MSD 5973 system with capillary column; Hewlett-Packard) with HFB anhydride derivatization

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Basal glycerol rate of appearance (R_a) in plasma, an indicator of whole body lipolytic rate, was greater in women than in men (2.1±0.2 vs. 1.5±0.1 mmol·kg body wt⁻¹·min⁻¹; P < 0.05)
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Basal glycerol rate of appearance (Ra) in plasma, an indicator of whole body lipolytic rate, was greater in women than in men (2.1±0.2 vs. 1.5±0.1 mmol·kg body wt−1·min−1; P < 0.05)

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: the relative increase in glycerol R_a with continued fasting was blunted in women compared with men (40±7 vs. 80±4% increase; P < 0.05)
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: the relative increase in glycerol Ra with continued fasting was blunted in women compared with men (40±7 vs. 80±4% increase; P < 0.05)

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: The relative increase in glycerol rate of appearance with continued fasting was blunted in women compared with men

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: glucose R_a was similar in men and women at 14 h (11±0.6 vs. 12±0.7 mmol·kg body wt⁻¹·min⁻¹) and 22 h of fasting (9±0.6 vs. 10±0.6 mmol·kg body wt⁻¹·min⁻¹)
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: glucose Ra was similar in men and women at 14 h (11±0.6 vs. 12±0.7 mmol·kg body wt−1·min−1) and 22 h of fasting (9±0.6 vs. 10±0.6 mmol·kg body wt−1·min−1)

gender difference

structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Plasma insulin concentration was significantly lower in women than in men at 22 h of fasting

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Plasma epinephrine concentration was twice as high in women than in men at 14 hours of fasting
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Mean basal plasma epinephrine concentration was twice as high in women than in men

aligned_method_pairs.pass_1_summary: Statistical analysis method | Two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with gender and time as factors, followed by post hoc Student's t-test for multiple comparisons; P ≤ 0.05 considered statistically significant | Two-way ANOVA
aligned_method_pairs.pass_2_summary: Statistical analysis method | Two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with gender and time as factors, followed by post hoc comparisons with Student's t-test for multiple comparisons | ANOVA

aligned_method_pairs.pass_1_summary: Fasting duration - study timepoint 1 | 14 hours of fasting | hours
Fasting duration - study timepoint 2 | 22 hours of fasting | hours
Subject positioning during study | Subjects remained in bed for the entire duration of the study
Participant N and demographics | Six men (32 ± 4 yr old) and six premenopausal women (27 ± 2 yr old), matched on percent body fat
aligned_method_pairs.pass_2_summary: Participant N and demographics | Six men (32 ± 4 yr old) and six premenopausal women (27 ± 2 yr old), matched on percent body fat
Blood sample collection tubes - insulin and glucagon | Chilled tubes containing EDTA and Trasylol to measure insulin and glucagon concentrations | Blood sampling
Blood sample collection tubes - catecholamines | Chilled tubes containing reduced glutathione and EGTA to determine plasma catecholamine concentrations | Blood sampling
Plasma separation timing | Plasma was separated by centrifugation within 30 minutes of collection | minutes
Plasma storage temperature | Plasma was stored at -70°C until final analyses were performed | °C
Statistical significance threshold | P ≤ 0.05

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: The relative increase in glycerol rate of appearance with continued fasting was blunted in women compared with men [gender difference]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: The relative increase in glycerol Ra with continued fasting was blunted in women compared with men [gender difference]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Glucose rate of appearance was similar in men and women at both 14 and 22 hours of fasting
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: Glucose Ra was similar in men and women at both 14 h and 22 h of fasting [no gender difference]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Sexual dimorphism exists in lipid, but not glucose, metabolism during both basal and short-term fasting conditions
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: Sexual dimorphism exists in lipid, but not glucose, metabolism during both basal and short-term fasting conditions [gender difference]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Plasma insulin concentration was significantly lower in women than in men at 22 hours of fasting [gender difference]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: Plasma insulin concentration was significantly lower in women than in men at 22 h of fasting [gender difference]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Plasma epinephrine concentration was twice as high in women than in men at 14 hours of fasting [gender difference]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: Mean basal plasma epinephrine concentration was twice as high in women than in men [gender difference]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Basal glycerol rate of appearance in plasma was greater in women than in men [gender difference]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: Basal glycerol rate of appearance in plasma was greater in women than in men [gender difference]
At 14 h of fasting, mean plasma fatty acid and glycerol concentrations were higher in women than in men [gender difference]

semantic_groups.rationale: Both describe identical inclusion criteria for health status with negligible wording difference ('considered to be in' vs 'considered in')
Both extractions are identical regarding medications and smoking inclusion criteria
Pass 1 only mentions body weight stability; Pass 2 adds exercise sedentary requirement that is missing from Pass 1's extraction of this criterion
Pass 1 describes exercise level criteria while Pass 2 describes menstrual cycle phase criteria - these are completely different inclusion criteria that should not be paired
Pass 1 contains menstrual cycle phase information but Pass 2 is empty, indicating this criterion was not extracted in Pass 2
Both describe the same body composition assessment method (DEXA/Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry); DEXA is standard abbreviation for the full term
Both describe identical pre-study meal timing and composition; minor abbreviation difference ('kcal/kg body weight' vs 'kcal/kg') is negligible
Both describe Ensure formula administration at 2000 with identical nutritional content; Pass 1 includes fasting instruction while Pass 2 omits it, but core fact about formula is equivalent
Pass 1 describes catheter insertion timing and procedure; Pass 2 describes blood collection tube specifications. These are different aspects of the methodology with no direct overlap.

Both describe the same glucose isotope infusion protocol with identical key parameters: [6,6-2H2]glucose, priming dose 18 mmol/kg, infusion rate 0.22 mmol/kg body weight/min, duration 210 minutes. Minor wording differences only.

Both describe the same glycerol isotope infusion protocol with identical parameters: [1,1,2,3,3-2H5]glycerol, priming dose 1.8 mmol/kg, infusion rate 0.12 mmol/kg body weight/min, duration 90 minutes. Minor wording differences only.
Both describe the first measurement period with 14 hours of fasting and blood sampling every 10 minutes between 0930 and 1000. Minor wording differences in presentation.
Both describe the second measurement period with 22 hours of fasting and blood sampling every 10 minutes between 1730 and 1800. Minor wording differences in presentation.
Both state insulin measurement was performed using radioimmunoassay. Identical fact with only minor wording variation in labels.
Both state glucagon measurement was performed using radioimmunoassay. Identical fact with only minor wording variation in labels.

Both describe catecholamine measurement using radioenzymatic method. Pass 1 includes 'single isotope derivative' while Pass 2 omits 'single isotope derivative' but both refer to the same assay method.

Both describe glucose measurement using the same automated analyzer (Yellow Springs Instrument). Minor wording differences ('measurement' vs 'assay method', 'Automated glucose analyzer' vs 'Automated analyzer') do not affect the underlying fact.

Both describe glycerol concentration measurement using GC-MS with [2-13C]glycerol as internal standard. Wording differences ('with' vs 'after...was added') and abbreviation variations (GC-MS vs full name) are negligible.

Both describe fatty acid concentration measurement using gas chromatography (Hewlett-Packard 5890-II) with heptadecanoic acid as internal standard. Wording differences ('with' vs 'after adding') are minor.

Both describe glycerol and glucose tracer-to-tracee ratio determination using GC-MS with HFB derivative formation. Pass_1 specifies 'EI ionization' while pass_2 omits this detail, but the core method is the same.

Both describe two-way ANOVA with gender and time as factors, followed by post hoc Student's t-test. Minor wording differences and pass_1's explicit significance threshold (P ≤ 0.05) do not constitute a conflict.
Pass_1 contains information about 14 hours fasting duration at study timepoint 1, but pass_2 is empty. This is an orphan entry.
Pass_1 contains information about 22 hours fasting duration at study timepoint 2, but pass_2 is empty. This is an orphan entry.
Pass_1 contains information about subject positioning (remained in bed), but pass_2 is empty. This is an orphan entry.
Identical text describing participant demographics with matching ages and matching criteria
Information present only in pass_2; pass_1 is empty. Details blood collection tubes for insulin and glucagon measurement
Information present only in pass_2; pass_1 is empty. Details blood collection tubes for catecholamine measurement
Information present only in pass_2; pass_1 is empty. Specifies plasma separation timing of 30 minutes
Information present only in pass_2; pass_1 is empty. Specifies plasma storage temperature of -70°C
Information present only in pass_2; pass_1 is empty. Specifies statistical significance threshold of P ≤ 0.05
Both describe the same finding: glycerol rate of appearance increase is blunted in women vs men during fasting. 'glycerol rate of appearance' and 'glycerol Ra' are equivalent abbreviations.
Both convey identical information: glucose Ra similar between genders at 14h and 22h fasting. Minor abbreviation differences (Ra vs rate of appearance, h vs hours) are negligible.
Identical statements about sexual dimorphism in lipid but not glucose metabolism. Pass_2 adds a tag but the factual content is the same.
Both state plasma insulin is significantly lower in women than men at 22h fasting. Only difference is '22 hours' vs '22 h' abbreviation.

Pass_1 specifies epinephrine is twice as high in women at 14h fasting. Pass_2 states 'mean basal' epinephrine without time specification. These describe different conditions (14h fasting vs basal), creating a factual conflict.
Identical statements about basal glycerol rate of appearance being greater in women than men.
Pass_2 contains a finding about fatty acid and glycerol concentrations at 14h fasting that has no corresponding entry in Pass_1.
